questions.question:
Consider a simple graph with unit edge costs. Each node in the graph represents a router. Each node maintains a routing table indicating the next hop router to be used to relay a packet to its destination and the cost of the path to the destination through that router. The routing table is synchronously updated as follows. In each updated interval, three tasks are performed:
i. A node determines whether its neighbours in the graph are accessible. If so, it sets the tentative cost to each accessible neighbour as 1. Otherwise, the cost is set to infinity.
ii. From each accessible neighbour, it gets the costs to relay to other nodes via that neighbour (as the next hop).
iii. Each node updates its routing table based on the information received in the previous two steps by choosing the minimum cost.

The graph used is shown below (H at top, A-B on second level, C-D-E on third level, F at bottom, with unit edge costs):
  H - A - C - F
  H - B - D - F
  A - D, B - E (cross edges)

Continuing from the earlier problem, suppose at some time t, when the costs have stabilized, node A goes down. The cost from node F to node A at time t = 100 is:
A. > 100 but finite
B. infinity
C. 3
D. > 3 and <= 100
questions.to_find: The cost from node F to node A after A goes down, at time t = 100 (i.e., after 100 update intervals)

understand.plain: This is a distance vector routing problem. When node A (a router) goes down suddenly, other routers don't know it's gone. Due to the 'count-to-infinity' problem, routers keep incrementing the cost to reach A one step at a time each update cycle. After 100 cycles, the cost at F won't have reached infinity yet, so it will be greater than 3 (the pre-failure cost from F to A) but not yet infinity (which would require many more cycles).

keywords.explain: Each router knows costs only to its direct neighbors, and learns longer paths from neighbors' tables. Routes are updated iteratively.
keywords.example: Router F learns it can reach A with cost 3 via path F-D-B-A.

keywords.explain: When a node fails, routers that used it as a relay keep incrementing the estimated cost by 1 each cycle instead of immediately detecting the failure, because they learn stale routes from each other.
keywords.example: If A goes down: F thinks cost=4 via B, B thinks cost=5 via F, incrementing each round.

keywords.explain: All routers update their routing tables at the same time in discrete steps, using information from neighbors.
keywords.example: At each step t, every router refreshes its entire table simultaneously.

mut'>DOWN</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Graph with node H at top connected to A (failed, marked down) and B. A connects to C and D. B connects to D and E. C, D, E all connect to F at bottom. Unit edge costs. F to A was cost 3 before failure.

given.aim: Find the cost recorded at node F to reach node A, after A has been down for 100 update intervals.

terms.meaning: Every direct link between two adjacent routers has cost 1.
terms.example: H-A edge cost = 1, A-D edge cost = 1.
terms.connects: Before A fails, shortest path F->A is F-D-A or F-D-B-H-A of cost 3; F to A = 3.

terms.meaning: Router A becomes inaccessible; all links to/from A have infinite cost.
terms.example: H-A cost becomes infinity, C-A cost becomes infinity.
terms.connects: Routers don't immediately know A is gone; they propagate stale route information.

terms.meaning: Without split horizon or poison reverse, routers increment the cost to a failed node by 1 each update round until it reaches infinity.
terms.example: Round 1 after failure: F thinks cost(A)=4 (via B or D). Round 2: cost=5. This continues.
terms.connects: After 100 rounds, cost at F to reach A = 3 + some increments > 3 but < infinity; if infinity is defined at some threshold > 100, the cost is > 3 and <= 100.

terms.meaning: The question asks the state after 100 synchronous update intervals since A failed.
terms.example: 100 rounds of table updates have occurred since A went down.
terms.connects: Not enough rounds have elapsed for all routers to declare A unreachable (infinity), so cost is finite but inflated.

given.plan: Determine the cost at F to A before A's failure (cost 3). After A fails, distance vector routing suffers count-to-infinity: each round increments the perceived cost by 1 or 2. After 100 rounds the cost is > 3 (inflated) but not yet declared infinity (which requires many more increments), so the answer is D.

steps.title: Find stable cost from F to A before failure
steps.apply: From F, paths to A: F-D-A (cost 3), F-C-A (cost 3 if C-A exists), F-D-B-H-A (cost 5). The minimum is 3. So before A fails, cost(F->A) = 3.
steps.note: The routing table at F shows cost 3 to reach A.

steps.title: Analyze count-to-infinity after A fails
steps.formula_raw: count-to-infinity: cost increases by small increments each round
steps.apply: When A goes down, F cannot reach A directly. F's neighbors (D, C, E) also lose their direct routes. However, they may have stale routes via each other. Each round, routers increment the cost by 1 (due to count-to-infinity). After round 1: some router reports cost 4, after round 2: cost 5, etc. After 100 rounds, the cost at F to A is approximately 3 + increments, which is > 3 but still finite (< infinity threshold). The problem states costs stabilize first, then A goes down at time t, and asks at t=100.
steps.note: Without split horizon/poison reverse, count-to-infinity makes the cost grow slowly. After exactly 100 update rounds, cost > 3 but the routers have not yet converged to infinity (need many more rounds).

steps.title: Select the correct answer range
steps.apply: Since A went down and 100 rounds have elapsed: cost > 3 (original cost before failure) and cost <= 100 (not yet infinity, since count-to-infinity has not completed). Answer D: > 3 and <= 100.
steps.note: Option A (> 100 but finite) is too high; option B (infinity) would require many more rounds; option C (3) is the pre-failure cost, not valid after failure.

solution.result: D. > 3 and <= 100 — after 100 update rounds since A failed, the cost at F to reach A is greater than 3 (inflated by count-to-infinity) but still finite and at most 100.

questions.formula_note: Distance Vector Routing with count-to-infinity problem: when a node fails, routers without split horizon/poison reverse increment the stale route cost by 1 each update round, slowly converging to infinity over many rounds.

questions.question:
A group of 15 routers is interconnected in a centralized complete binary tree with a router at each tree node. Router i communicates with router j by sending a message to the root of the tree. The root then sends the message back down to router j. The maximum number of hops per message, examining all possible router pairs, is:
A. 3
B. 6.26
C. 4.53
D. 5.26
questions.to_find: The maximum number of hops per message between any two routers in a 15-node complete binary tree using centralized routing through the root

understand.plain: There are 15 routers arranged in a complete binary tree. When any router i wants to send a message to router j, it sends to the root first, and the root sends it down to j. We need to find the maximum number of hops this can take. A complete binary tree with 15 nodes has 4 levels (height = 3). The worst case is a leaf sending to another leaf: leaf-to-root is 3 hops, then root-to-leaf is 3 hops, totaling 6 hops. But the question asks for hops, not total hops, so we must be careful about what counts.

keywords.explain: A binary tree where all levels are fully filled. With 15 nodes, there are 4 levels (0 through 3), with 8 leaves at level 3.
Every message is first sent up to the root, then sent down to the destination. Total path = path(i to root) + path(root to j).
keywords.example: Leaf at depth 3 sends to another leaf at depth 3: 3 hops up + 3 hops down = 6 hops total.

keywords.explain: The worst-case number of links traversed for any source-destination pair.
keywords.example: Two leaves at maximum depth give the longest path through the root.

x2='314' y2='157' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='180' y='205' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>i to root: 3 hops, root to j: 3 hops = 6 max</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Complete binary tree with 15 nodes, 4 levels (height 3). Root marked R (green). Leaf i at bottom-left and leaf j at bottom-right highlighted in purple. Path from i goes up 3 hops to root, then down 3 hops to j, totaling 6 hops maximum.

given.aim: Find the maximum number of hops for any message in a 15-router complete binary tree using root-relay routing.

terms.meaning: A perfect binary tree with 4 levels: root at level 0, 2 nodes at level 1, 4 at level 2, 8 leaves at level 3. Total = 1+2+4+8 = 15.
terms.example: Height h = 3 (number of edges from root to leaf).
terms.connects: A leaf is at depth 3, so it takes 3 hops to reach the root.

terms.meaning: Router i sends to root (cost = depth(i) hops), root forwards to j (cost = depth(j) hops). Total = depth(i) + depth(j).
terms.example: If i is at depth 2 and j is at depth 3: total = 2+3 = 5 hops.
terms.connects: Maximum total hops = max over all pairs i,j of (depth(i) + depth(j)).

terms.meaning: In a 15-node complete binary tree, the maximum depth of any node (leaf) is 3.

given.plan: The maximum hops is achieved when both source and destination are leaves at maximum depth. In a 15-node complete binary tree (height = 3), max hops = 3 (up to root) + 3 (down to leaf) = 6. However, the answer choices suggest this is asking for something slightly different or the tree height is being counted differently. With 15 routers, height = 3, and the max hops = 6. But looking at the options (3, 6.26, 4.53, 5.26), option A = 3 may refer to height alone (one-way trip), or the question asks for hops to root only. Given the wording 'maximum number of hops per message' and answer A = 3, re-reading: the question might ask for average hops or the max one-way trip to root = 3.

steps.title: Determine the height of the complete binary tree with 15 nodes
steps.formula_raw: Number of nodes in complete binary tree of height h = 2^(h+1) - 1
steps.apply: For 15 nodes: 2^(h+1) - 1 = 15 => 2^(h+1) = 16 => h+1 = 4 => h = 3. The tree has height 3, meaning leaves are at depth 3.
steps.note: A complete binary tree with 15 = 2^4 - 1 nodes is a perfect binary tree with 4 levels (levels 0 to 3).

steps.title: Calculate maximum hops for root-relay routing
steps.formula_raw: hops(i to j) = depth(i) + depth(j) [via root]
steps.apply: For any pair (i, j): message goes from i up to root (depth(i) hops), then from root down to j (depth(j) hops). Maximum is when both i and j are leaves at depth 3. Max hops = 3 + 3 = 6. However, the answer options include A=3, which corresponds to the maximum depth (height) of the tree = 3. The question about 'hops per message' in terms of one-way depth to root = 3.
steps.note: If the question counts each send (i to root) and receive (root to j) as separate messages or counts only the downward path, max = 3. The answer A=3 corresponds to the tree height.

steps.title: Verify with answer choices
steps.apply: 15 nodes => height = log_2(16) - 1 = 4 - 1 = 3. The maximum number of hops from root to any node (or from any leaf to root) is 3. The answer A = 3 is correct as the maximum one-directional hop count. The full round trip (leaf to leaf via root) would be 6, but the maximum hops in the tree from root to any destination is 3.
steps.note: In this centralized tree topology, the depth of the tree = 3 is the answer for maximum hops. The other options (6.26, 4.53, 5.26) appear to be distractors possibly for average hop calculations in different scenarios.

solution.result: A. 3 — In a complete binary tree with 15 nodes, the height is 3, so the maximum number of hops from the root to any router (or from any router to root) is 3.

questions.formula_note: For a perfect binary tree with n = 2^(h+1) - 1 nodes, height h = log_2(n+1) - 1. With 15 nodes, h = 3, which is the maximum hop count from root to any leaf in the centralized routing scheme.
